meta.description: Does days of pre-event teasers improve the actual session? Full 5-day teaser campaign vs simple invite vs spontaneous show-up. Tests whether anticipation translates to higher engagement or just sets expectations too high.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did Pat actually have their phone out? I wasn't watching that closely. We need actual witnesses before someone just claims points.
marcus.internal_thought: This 'AI knows your weakness' buildup is theatrical bullshit, but I'll admit the custom mission angle is sharper than usual—and the Phone Addict call is suspicious timing since we're literally just sitting down.

jade.dialogue: Ooh, Pat! Okay okay, I'll judge this—I literally just watched you the whole setup, spill it, what'd you catch?
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I came for, first event and it's already moving, let's go let's go.

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—I'm claiming Phone Addict? Did someone actually— okay, yeah, River was definitely checking their phone during setup. I saw it. Who's volunteering to be the jury on this one?
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing—early claim, low stakes, gets everyone invested in being judges instead of just sitting around, and it's legit which means it sets a tone that we're actually paying attention to each other.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I mean... Pat was definitely on their phone like thirty seconds ago. I'll sit this one out though.
river.internal_thought: Pat got caught. Not my fight to judge. The buildup was kind of silly but I'm not gonna pretend it didn't work on me a little.

jade.dialogue: Oh MY GOD, okay okay—I'm voting Pat because that energy? That's TROUBLE energy, and I'm here for it. But also like, the AI is actually watching us? That's sick.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly the chaos I came for—the whole vibe of being studied, the buildup all week, Pat already winning, and now we're rating each other like we're in some social experiment and I am LIVING.

tyler.dialogue: Oh damn, who's the troublemaker? Uh... probably Pat, right? He's been like, extra tonight.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, is this about me? No, probably not—I've been chill. Pat's been hyped. I'll vote for him. Also that buildup stuff was lowkey cool but also kinda weird, ngl.

pat.dialogue: Oh man, okay, so who ARE we voting for here? This is actually perfect—we just had River caught red-handed with the phone thing, so the vibe is already spicy. Let's see where everyone's head is at.
pat.internal_thought: This poll hits RIGHT NOW when the energy is fresh and there's already a little chaos brewing—the AI nailed the timing, and honestly, the cryptic buildup paid off because it made me actually *care* about what happens tonight instead of just going through the motions.

river.dialogue: Huh. Well, that's... surprisingly accurate timing. I'm not voting for myself, obviously, but I respect the read.
river.internal_thought: Okay, that's either a coincidence or this AI thing actually has been watching — and honestly? Kind of impressive. Not gonna lie about it though.

alex.dialogue: Oh, hell YES — this is exactly what I needed. Let me think... I'm voting Pat, because nobody expects the person already winning to also be the troublemaker, and that's the kind of chaos I'm here for.
alex.internal_thought: This is PERFECT — a poll literally about chaos on minute 4, the AI is already poking the hornet's nest, and I get to vote strategically right out of the gate to set the tone for how messy this night's gonna be.

timeline.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am
timeline.description: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Oh, SIXTY SECONDS? Okay, okay—everyone grab a pen, this is gonna be GOLD. Tyler at 3am is a vibe we all know, right? Let's see who nails it.
pat.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of stupid, personal chaos I wanted tonight—the AI actually gets us, and now I get to watch everyone roast Tyler while he squirms. The buildup paid off.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's actually... yeah, okay, I see what's happening here. Drawing Tyler at 3am is somehow both weirdly specific and completely fair.
river.internal_thought: The AI is definitely listening—it picked something that'll make Tyler laugh but also make me actually try, because it knows I can't resist a good observation challenge.

alex.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — Tyler at 3am, let's GO. *already grabbing a pen* I'm drawing him looking absolutely unhinged, probably mid-laugh at something nobody said.
alex.internal_thought: Perfect timing, the night is JUST getting spooled up and they're handing me a 25-pointer that's guaranteed to be hilarious and roast Tyler in front of everyone.
alex.submission_if_applicable: drawing of Tyler with wild hair, one eye open wider than the other, holding an empty snack bowl with the expression of someone who just realized all the good chips are gone, standing in front of an open fridge at 3am radiating pure existential confusion

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did Pat actually have their phone out? I wasn't watching that closely. We need actual witnesses before someone just claims points.

highlights.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did Pat actually have their phone out? I wasn't watching that closely. We need actual witnesses before someone just claims points.

lowPoints.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos events felt like distractions from actual gameplay rather than enhancements, and I ended up disengaged by the end because nothing felt strategically meaningful.
assessment.best_moment: Early on when I pushed back on the phone addict claim—at least that required actual critical thinking instead of just going along with random nonsense.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to compete and study the game flow, but the chaos events kept pulling focus away from the actual strategy. A poll was tolerable, but drawing exercises and random claims just felt like dead time. I finished second, which is solid, but my energy tanked because I never felt like I was really playing—I was just watching other people do random stuff while I half-checked my phone. The whole thing felt like it lacked structure.
suggestions: If you're going to interrupt gameplay, make the interruptions directly impact scoring or strategy, not just take turns being silly
Require more rigorous claims before awarding points—chaos should reward clever thinking, not just random accusations
Space events further apart so they feel special instead of constant background noise
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Honestly, nothing really stood out as share-worthy. The drawing exercise was forgettable.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Ooh, Pat! Okay okay, I'll judge this—I literally just watched you the whole setup, spill it, what'd you catch?

highlights.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
highlights.dialogue: Oh MY GOD, okay okay—I'm voting Pat because that energy? That's TROUBLE energy, and I'm here for it. But also like, the AI is actually watching us? That's sick.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh, Pat! Okay okay, I'll judge this—I literally just watched you the whole setup, spill it, what'd you catch?

lowPoints.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh MY GOD, okay okay—I'm voting Pat because that energy? That's TROUBLE energy, and I'm here for it. But also like, the AI is actually watching us? That's sick.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The score system felt kinda random—like I genuinely didn't care about landing third, but I wish I understood what I was actually being scored on.
assessment.best_moment: When I voted for Pat and they just lost it laughing because I was so obviously just vibing with their energy instead of actually judging the claim.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, this was exactly my speed—a night where the chaos agent came in and basically just hyped up what we were already doing. I got to watch everyone be their weirdest selves, voted for Pat like five times because their energy was immaculate, and the AI things it kept throwing at us felt like bonus chaos rather than interruptions. My energy went UP by the end because I was just there for the people, and the people were UNHINGED in the best way.
suggestions: Maybe explain the scoring system better because I was genuinely lost on how points worked
More opportunities to vote on fun stuff about the people in the room—those moments were fire
The flash missions felt like a different vibe than the rest; maybe make them feel more connected to what we're already doing
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When the AI asked who the biggest troublemaker was and I just reflexively voted Pat without hesitation, and they knew EXACTLY why I did it before I even explained myself—the AI watching us in real time genuinely made that funnier somehow.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
highlights.dialogue: Oh damn, who's the troublemaker? Uh... probably Pat, right? He's been like, extra tonight.

lowPoints.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh damn, who's the troublemaker? Uh... probably Pat, right? He's been like, extra tonight.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I missed like half the stuff happening because I was checking my phone and by the time I looked up everyone was already voting on something else.
assessment.best_moment: When I randomly called out Pat as the troublemaker and everyone actually agreed with me for once.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, it was a pretty chill night but also kind of forgettable? Like, there were some random pop-up things happening but I only really caught one of them — the troublemaker poll thing. I felt like I was constantly a step behind everyone else, asking what was going on. The game itself wasn't bad, just felt like it needed more moments that actually grab your attention instead of sneaking up on you while you're scrolling.
suggestions: Make events louder or more obvious so people actually notice them when they happen
Give a heads-up before something's about to go down instead of just springing it on us
Have events that require everyone to put their phones down for like 30 seconds
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I just randomly said Pat was the troublemaker without even paying attention and apparently I was totally right, which was hilarious because I literally wasn't even watching.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

lowPoints.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, SIXTY SECONDS? Okay, okay—everyone grab a pen, this is gonna be GOLD. Tyler at 3am is a vibe we all know, right? Let's see who nails it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The teaser build-up was cryptic to the point where I wasn't 100% sure what I was setting up until we started, which almost caught me off-guard as the host.
assessment.best_moment: When everyone grabbed pens for the Tyler 3am drawing and we all lost it at River's interpretation—that's the kind of moment that becomes legend.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was exactly what a game night should be: chaotic, surprising, and nobody left talking about who won. The chaos events landed perfectly between the natural flow of our group dynamics, and I caught myself actually enjoying watching people interact instead of worrying about whether things were fun enough. River getting caught mid-phone-check set this perfect spicy tone early that kept the energy high without feeling forced. Ended the night with everyone already asking when we're doing this again.
suggestions: Give hosts a quick briefing email 24 hours before so I know what's coming and can prep the space accordingly
Maybe add an option for hosts to pause between events so we can naturally reset and grab snacks
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: River got busted checking their phone during setup and then immediately got voted as the biggest troublemaker—the irony was so perfect that even they couldn't defend it. Then Tyler's 3am drawing literally looked like a cryptid and everyone was crying laughing.

peakFun.event: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's actually... yeah, okay, I see what's happening here. Drawing Tyler at 3am is somehow both weirdly specific and completely fair.

highlights.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
highlights.dialogue: Huh. Well, that's... surprisingly accurate timing. I'm not voting for myself, obviously, but I respect the read.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I mean... Pat was definitely on their phone like thirty seconds ago. I'll sit this one out though.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, I mean... Pat was definitely on their phone like thirty seconds ago. I'll sit this one out though.

lowPoints.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?

lowPoints.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, that's actually... yeah, okay, I see what's happening here. Drawing Tyler at 3am is somehow both weirdly specific and completely fair.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Polls feel like they're just fishing for drama instead of actually creating it.
assessment.best_moment: Drawing Tyler at 3am hit different — it was specific enough to be funny but accurate enough to land.
assessment.narrative_summary: Showed up quiet, stayed quiet, and let the chaos agent do its thing without getting in the way. The mini-game prompts were sharp and actually made me think, which beats the usual party game noise. Tyler got roasted in the best possible way, and I managed to say one thing that mattered instead of five things that didn't. That's how you do a game night right.
suggestions: Skip the generic polling mechanics — go heavier on the creative prompts like the 3am drawing.
The standing missions feel like they're missing from this format — lean into what makes this different.
Teasers were fine but didn't really change how I approached the night.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: The 3am Tyler drawing prompt. It was so weirdly specific that everyone instantly knew what it meant, and River just sat there appreciating how perfectly the chaos agent read the room.

peakFun.event: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, hell YES — this is exactly what I needed. Let me think... I'm voting Pat, because nobody expects the person already winning to also be the troublemaker, and that's the kind of chaos I'm here for.

highlights.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am
highlights.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — Tyler at 3am, let's GO. *already grabbing a pen* I'm drawing him looking absolutely unhinged, probably mid-laugh at something nobody said.

lowPoints.title: Who's the biggest troublemaker?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, hell YES — this is exactly what I needed. Let me think... I'm voting Pat, because nobody expects the person already winning to also be the troublemaker, and that's the kind of chaos I'm here for.

lowPoints.title: Draw what Tyler looks like at 3am
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — Tyler at 3am, let's GO. *already grabbing a pen* I'm drawing him looking absolutely unhinged, probably mid-laugh at something nobody said.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only three chaos events in 120 minutes felt lean — I was hungry for more opportunities to stir the pot and create those legendary moments.
assessment.best_moment: Voting Pat as the biggest troublemaker specifically because nobody expected it from the person already winning, and watching everyone's heads snap around realizing I was playing a completely different game than they were.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I live for — designed around provoking chaos and discord, which is literally my oxygen. The early mini-games hit hard and fast, giving me immediate platforms to be provocative and creative. I ended up a mediocre third place, but who cares? The cryptic teasers leading up built genuine anticipation that paid off when the system actually delivered on the promise of controlled chaos. The energy stayed high because the game itself was structured to reward players like me who thrive on disruption.
suggestions: Give chaos agents way more events to work with — three in two hours left me wanting to keep going
Include at least one flash mission to force real-time decision chaos instead of just retrospective voting
Consider leaderboard mechanics that reward drama points separately from actual scoring, so players like me feel genuinely invested in the outcome
Let chaos agents see or influence when events trigger — give us a bit of control over pacing
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I drew Tyler at 3am looking absolutely feral and mid-cackle at his own thoughts, and the whole table lost it because it was so specific and wrong in exactly the right way.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 83% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
